questionText: You are building an e-commerce platform. The system must use a single shared payment-gateway client throughout the whole application, and it must also be able to create whole families of related UI widgets (buttons, checkboxes, menus) styled consistently for either the 'light' or 'dark' storefront theme. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: A single shared instance is Singleton; creating coordinated families of related products (themed widget sets) is Abstract Factory.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that DatabaseConnection is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: Match each design pattern to the intent that best describes it.
matchingOptions: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly
Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
Encapsulates object creation in one method or class that returns instances based on a parameter

correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Flyweight: Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
correctAnswers.Mediator: Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly
correctAnswers.Simple Factory: Encapsulates object creation in one method or class that returns instances based on a parameter
correctAnswers.Strategy: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: In an online banking app, every dashboard widget must refresh automatically whenever the account balance changes. Separately, each user action (transfer, deposit, withdrawal) must be encapsulated so it can be logged, queued, and undone. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Auto-refreshing dependents on a state change is Observer; encapsulating each action as a queueable/undoable object is Command.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that ConfigManager is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: Group the following design patterns with their defining intent.
matchingOptions: Defines a grammar and an interpreter that evaluates sentences written in that language
Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently
Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state all its dependents are notified automatically
Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it
Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses

correctAnswers.Bridge: Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently
correctAnswers.Decorator: Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it
correctAnswers.Interpreter: Defines a grammar and an interpreter that evaluates sentences written in that language
correctAnswers.Observer: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state all its dependents are notified automatically
correctAnswers.Template Method: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A smart-home controller must let users press one 'Good Night' button that turns off lights, locks doors and lowers the thermostat through many different vendor APIs, exposing a single simple call to the app. Each individual device action must also be undoable. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: A single simplified entry point over many subsystems is Facade; undoable encapsulated device actions are Command.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that Logger is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: For each design pattern below, select the statement that captures its purpose.
matchingOptions: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
Composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly

correctAnswers.Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Composite: Composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
correctAnswers.Facade: Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
correctAnswers.Factory Method: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A ride-sharing app must switch its fare-calculation algorithm at runtime (normal, surge, off-peak) without changing the booking code. Which single design pattern is the most appropriate?
explanation: Interchangeable, runtime-selectable algorithms behind a common interface is the Strategy pattern.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that CachePool is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: Associate every design pattern with the goal it is meant to achieve.
matchingOptions: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly
Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo

correctAnswers.Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Factory Method: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
correctAnswers.Flyweight: Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
correctAnswers.Mediator: Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A hospital records system must check a user's permissions before any access to a sensitive patient file is allowed, returning a local error otherwise. It must also let staff browse departments, sub-departments and individual patients through one uniform tree interface. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: An access-control gatekeeper in front of the real object is Proxy; treating individual records and groups of records uniformly in a tree is Composite.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that AuditTrail is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: Match each design pattern to the intent that best describes it.
matchingOptions: Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently
Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it
Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class

correctAnswers.Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
correctAnswers.Bridge: Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently
correctAnswers.Chain of Responsibility: Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
correctAnswers.Facade: Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
correctAnswers.Proxy: Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it
correctAnswers.State: Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: In a forest-rendering game, millions of trees share the same mesh and texture data to save memory, while each tree still has its own position and scale. The studio also wants to spawn new pre-configured enemy types by copying an existing template instance instead of rebuilding them. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Sharing intrinsic state across many objects while keeping extrinsic state outside is Flyweight; cloning a pre-configured template is Prototype.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that IdGenerator is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: Group the following design patterns with their defining intent.
matchingOptions: Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it

correctAnswers.Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
correctAnswers.Chain of Responsibility: Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Decorator: Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A social-media backend must notify all of a user's followers when that user posts, and each notification must be deliverable through interchangeable channels (email, SMS, push) chosen at runtime. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Notifying many followers on a change is Observer; swapping the delivery algorithm at runtime is Strategy.

questionText: Fill in the blanks so that ThreadPool is a correct (lazy) Singleton.
explanation: Private constructor blocks outside instantiation, a static accessor lazily creates and returns the single instance.

questionText: For each design pattern below, select the statement that captures its purpose.
matchingOptions: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state all its dependents are notified automatically
Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class
Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime

correctAnswers.Facade: Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
correctAnswers.Observer: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state all its dependents are notified automatically
correctAnswers.State: Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class
correctAnswers.Strategy: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A cloud file system must let users treat files and folders uniformly (a folder's size is the sum of its contents), and must let them dynamically wrap any file with optional features such as encryption and compression, stackable in any order. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Uniform treatment of leaves and containers in a tree is Composite; stacking optional responsibilities by wrapping is Decorator.

questionText: Complete the object Adapter so PaymentProcessorAdapter exposes LegacyBank through the PaymentProcessor interface.
explanation: The adapter implements the target interface and delegates to the adaptee's real method.

questionText: Associate every design pattern with the goal it is meant to achieve.
matchingOptions: Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it
Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects
Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently

correctAnswers.Bridge: Decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently
correctAnswers.Chain of Responsibility: Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
correctAnswers.Factory Method: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
correctAnswers.Proxy: Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it
correctAnswers.Visitor: Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A logging framework must guarantee exactly one logger instance application-wide, and must pass each log record through an ordered chain of handlers (console, file, alerting) where each handler decides whether to process and/or forward it. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: One global instance is Singleton; an ordered set of handlers each forwarding to the next is Chain of Responsibility.

questionText: Complete the object Adapter so MediaPlayerAdapter exposes Mp4Library through the MediaPlayer interface.
explanation: The adapter implements the target interface and delegates to the adaptee's real method.

questionText: Match each design pattern to the intent that best describes it.
matchingOptions: Provides an interface for creating families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes
Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly
Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point to it
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect

correctAnswers.Abstract Factory: Provides an interface for creating families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes
correctAnswers.Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Flyweight: Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
correctAnswers.Mediator: Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly
correctAnswers.Singleton: Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point to it

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A plugin system loads third-party extensions that all implement a common Plugin interface; the concrete plugin class to instantiate is decided by subclasses of the host application. Each loaded plugin can additionally be wrapped to add logging around its calls without changing the plugin. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Letting subclasses decide which concrete product to create is Factory Method; transparently wrapping to add behavior is Decorator.

questionText: Complete the object Adapter so PrinterAdapter exposes OldPrinter through the Printer interface.
explanation: The adapter implements the target interface and delegates to the adaptee's real method.

questionText: Group the following design patterns with their defining intent.
matchingOptions: Composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point to it
Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses

correctAnswers.Composite: Composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
correctAnswers.Flyweight: Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
correctAnswers.Singleton: Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point to it
correctAnswers.Template Method: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A multi-tenant SaaS app must hold exactly one configuration object per running instance, and that configuration must be assembled step by step from many optional settings to avoid a giant telescoping constructor. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: One config per instance is Singleton; assembling a complex object via a step-by-step fluent API is Builder.

questionText: Complete the object Adapter so NotifierAdapter exposes EmailLib through the Notifier interface.
explanation: The adapter implements the target interface and delegates to the adaptee's real method.

questionText: For each design pattern below, select the statement that captures its purpose.
matchingOptions: Composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
Separates the construction of a complex object from its representation using a step-by-step process
Encapsulates object creation in one method or class that returns instances based on a parameter

correctAnswers.Builder: Separates the construction of a complex object from its representation using a step-by-step process
correctAnswers.Chain of Responsibility: Passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it
correctAnswers.Composite: Composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
correctAnswers.Factory Method: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
correctAnswers.Simple Factory: Encapsulates object creation in one method or class that returns instances based on a parameter

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A report-generation module always follows the same fixed steps — fetch data, format, export — and this order must never change, although the formatting and export steps differ per report type. Optionally, a watermark and a header can be layered on top of any report without altering the core flow. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: A fixed algorithm skeleton with overridable steps is Template Method; optional add-ons layered without touching the core flow are Decorator.

questionText: Complete the object Adapter so LoggerAdapter exposes Log4jLib through the Logger interface.
explanation: The adapter implements the target interface and delegates to the adaptee's real method.

questionText: Associate every design pattern with the goal it is meant to achieve.
matchingOptions: Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects
Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime
Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class
Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it

correctAnswers.Flyweight: Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
correctAnswers.Proxy: Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it
correctAnswers.State: Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class
correctAnswers.Strategy: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime
correctAnswers.Visitor: Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: Your app integrates a third-party payment gateway whose API does not match your internal PaymentService interface, so you must wrap it without changing your existing code. In addition, before any charge is sent you must run a local fraud check that can block the request before it reaches the gateway. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Wrapping a mismatched third-party API behind your expected interface is Adapter; intercepting and possibly blocking the call locally is Proxy.

questionText: Complete the object Adapter so DataExporterAdapter exposes CsvWriterOld through the DataExporter interface.
explanation: The adapter implements the target interface and delegates to the adaptee's real method.

questionText: Match each design pattern to the intent that best describes it.
matchingOptions: Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it
Defines a grammar and an interpreter that evaluates sentences written in that language
Separates the construction of a complex object from its representation using a step-by-step process
Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects
Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime
Provides sequential access to the elements of a collection without exposing its underlying representation

correctAnswers.Builder: Separates the construction of a complex object from its representation using a step-by-step process
correctAnswers.Decorator: Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it
correctAnswers.Interpreter: Defines a grammar and an interpreter that evaluates sentences written in that language
correctAnswers.Iterator: Provides sequential access to the elements of a collection without exposing its underlying representation
correctAnswers.Strategy: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime
correctAnswers.Visitor: Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A document editor must support unlimited undo/redo by capturing snapshots of the document's state and restoring them later, and each editing operation (insert, delete, format) must be encapsulated as an object. Which design patterns best fit? Select 1 or more answers
explanation: Capturing and restoring state snapshots is Memento; encapsulating each edit as an object (also enabling undo) is Command.

questionText: Complete the Factory Method: WindowsDialog must override the creator of Dialog.
explanation: The creator declares an abstract factory method; the concrete creator extends it and returns the concrete product.

questionText: Group the following design patterns with their defining intent.
matchingOptions: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
Encapsulates object creation in one method or class that returns instances based on a parameter
Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
Captures and externalizes an object's internal state so it can be restored later without violating encapsulation

correctAnswers.Factory Method: Defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate
correctAnswers.Flyweight: Shares common intrinsic state between many fine-grained objects to save memory
correctAnswers.Memento: Captures and externalizes an object's internal state so it can be restored later without violating encapsulation
correctAnswers.Simple Factory: Encapsulates object creation in one method or class that returns instances based on a parameter

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A caching layer should sit in front of a slow database, returning cached results when possible and only querying the database on a miss, transparently to the client which still uses the same Repository interface. Which single design pattern is the most appropriate?
explanation: A surrogate that controls access to the real object (here adding caching) while keeping the same interface is Proxy.

questionText: Complete the Factory Method: RoadLogistics must override the creator of Logistics.
explanation: The creator declares an abstract factory method; the concrete creator extends it and returns the concrete product.

questionText: For each design pattern below, select the statement that captures its purpose.
matchingOptions: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses
Provides an interface for creating families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes
Creates new objects by cloning an existing instance instead of instantiating from scratch
Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state all its dependents are notified automatically
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime

correctAnswers.Abstract Factory: Provides an interface for creating families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes
correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Observer: Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state all its dependents are notified automatically
correctAnswers.Prototype: Creates new objects by cloning an existing instance instead of instantiating from scratch
correctAnswers.Strategy: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and makes them selectable at runtime
correctAnswers.Template Method: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, deferring some steps to subclasses

explanation: Each pattern's intent is its textbook one-line purpose.

questionText: A thermostat in a smart-home app behaves completely differently depending on whether it is in Heating, Cooling or Off mode, and pressing the same button causes different transitions in each mode. Which single design pattern is the most appropriate?
explanation: An object that changes its behavior as its internal mode changes, appearing to change class, is the State pattern.

questionText: Complete the Factory Method: EspressoBar must override the creator of Cafe.
explanation: The creator declares an abstract factory method; the concrete creator extends it and returns the concrete product.

questionText: Associate every design pattern with the goal it is meant to achieve.
matchingOptions: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
Creates new objects by cloning an existing instance instead of instantiating from scratch
Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects
Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly

correctAnswers.Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
correctAnswers.Command: Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo
correctAnswers.Mediator: Centralizes communication between a set of objects so they no longer refer to each other directly
correctAnswers.Prototype: Creates new objects by cloning an existing instance instead of instantiating from scratch
correctAnswers.Visitor: Lets you define a new operation on a set of objects without changing the classes of those objects
